title: Sell-through Rate
when_to_use: assess inventory health and markdown timing
identify slow-moving stock requiring promotion
benchmark category or SKU sell-through efficiency
optimize purchase planning and reorder points
evaluate merchandising impact on inventory velocity
business_definition: Ratio of units sold to units available at the start of the period, expressed as a percentage. Units available at period start are calculated as (beginning_inventory - reserved_inventory + incoming_inventory) and units sold are completed sales in the period. Excludes cancelled, returned, or reserved units.

metric_nature.what_it_measures: How quickly inventory is converted to sales — a measure of demand vs. supply alignment and stock efficiency.
metric_nature.what_it_does_not_measure: Revenue or profit from sold units. High sell-through may occur with low-margin items; this metric tracks quantity, not value.
metric_nature.common_misconception: Sell-through rate is not the same as inventory turnover. Turnover = cost of goods sold ÷ average inventory; sell-through = units sold ÷ beginning inventory — the latter focuses on volume velocity, not financial value.

inflation_causes.cause: Using ending inventory instead of beginning inventory as denominator
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If inventory sold is divided by ending inventory (instead of beginning), and inventory levels are declining, the denominator shrinks — artificially inflating the rate (e.g., sell 100 of 50 ending = 200%).
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT inventory_date, SUM(quantity) AS ending_inventory, LAG(SUM(quantity)) OVER (ORDER BY inventory_date) AS prev_inventory FROM inventory_snapshots WHERE inventory_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY inventory_date DESC
inflation_causes.fix: Always use beginning inventory as the denominator: `units_sold / beginning_inventory`. End inventory can be used for *current* stock health, but not sell-through.

inflation_causes.cause: Including reserved or allocated inventory in beginning quantity
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If beginning inventory includes reserved units (e.g., pending orders, QTY held for holdouts), but those reserved units are not subtracted before sell-through, the denominator is inflated — deflating rate. However, if reserved units are later *sold*, the numerator inflates *more* — net effect: erratic spikes.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT sku_id, SUM(quantity) AS total_inventory, SUM(CASE WHEN allocation_type = 'reserved' THEN quantity END) AS reserved FROM inventory_snapshots WHERE snapshot_date = '2024-01-01' GROUP BY sku_id HAVING SUM(CASE WHEN allocation_type = 'reserved' THEN quantity END) > 0 ORDER BY reserved DESC LIMIT 10
inflation_causes.fix: Subtract reserved and allocated quantities before computing beginning inventory: `beginning = SUM(quantity) - SUM(CASE WHEN allocation_type IN ('reserved','allocated') THEN quantity END)`.

inflation_causes.cause: Counting units sold from cancelled orders
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If cancelled orders are included in units_sold (e.g., via order_item status not filtered), units sold inflates — artificially increasing sell-through rate.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT order_id, status, SUM(oi.quantity) AS items FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'cancelled' GROUP BY 1, 2 HAVING SUM(oi.quantity) > 0 ORDER BY items DESC LIMIT 10
inflation_causes.fix: Filter units_sold to only include orders with status = 'completed' and exclude refunds/reversals.

deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If inventory snapshots only capture beginning-of-day inventory, and new stock arrives *during* the period, those units are not included in beginning inventory — but their sell-through is included in units_sold — deflating sell-through rate.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT sku_id, SUM(CASE WHEN snapshot_date = '2024-01-01' THEN quantity END) AS beginning, SUM(CASE WHEN transaction_type = 'receipt' AND transaction_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31' THEN quantity END) AS incoming FROM inventory_transactions WHERE sku_id IN (SELECT sku_id FROM inventory_snapshots WHERE snapshot_date = '2024-01-01') GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY incoming DESC LIMIT 10
deflation_causes.fix: Include incoming inventory received *before or during* the period in beginning inventory: `beginning = beginning_snap + SUM(received_qty WHERE received_date <= end_date)`.

diagnostic_questions: Is the sell-through anomaly concentrated in specific SKUs, categories, or vendors?
Has a markdown promotion or clearance event been launched recently?
Are sell-through rates higher for new vs. established products?
Did inventory accuracy issues (e.g., shrinkage, system sync errors) affect beginning/ending counts?
Are units sold being double-counted (e.g., split shipments, partial fulfillment)?

causal_relationships.then: sustained demand — likely consistent popularity or effective merchandising
inventory optimization — likely better forecasting or earlier markdowns clearing slow-movers
inventory mismatch — likely sizing, quality, or demand forecasting issues causing returns and stalled velocity

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Sell-through rate drops >10% MoM with stable purchase orders
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: A shift in demand (e.g., changing trends, competitor新品) has made older inventory less desirable — both sell-through slows and dead stock accumulates.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', i.snapshot_date) AS month, SUM(i.quantity) FILTER (WHERE i.quantity > 0) AS begin_qty, SUM(oi.quantity) FILTER (WHERE o.status = 'completed') AS sold, SUM(oi.quantity) FILTER (WHERE o.status = 'completed') * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(i.quantity), 0) AS sell_through FROM inventory_snapshots i LEFT JOIN order_items oi ON i.sku_id = oi.product_id LEFT JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE i.snapshot_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-02-28' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY month

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Sell-through rate spikes during a flash sale
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: A deep discount promotion is clearing inventory quickly (high sell-through) but eroding margins — likely a trade-off between velocity and profitability.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', o.order_date) AS week, p.promotion_code, COUNT(DISTINCT oi.order_id) AS orders, SUM(oi.quantity) AS units, SUM(oi.quantity) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN oi.order_date = '2024-01-01' THEN oi.quantity END) OVER (PARTITION BY p.promotion_code), 0) AS sell_through, AVG(nr.net_revenue) AS avg_nrv FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id JOIN promotions pr ON p.promotion_id = pr.promotion_id JOIN net_revenue_daily nr ON oi.order_id = nr.order_id WHERE p.promotion_code IS NOT NULL AND o.order_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1, p.promotion_code ORDER BY week

anti_patterns.why_wrong: Averaging beginning and ending inventory is correct for *turnover ratio* (COGS ÷ avg inventory), but sell-through is a *flow-to-stock* metric and must use *beginning* stock only — averaging deflates the denominator and inflates rate.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT units_sold / NULLIF((beginning_inventory + ending_inventory) / 2, 0) AS rate FROM inventory_metrics
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT units_sold * 100.0 / NULLIF(beginning_inventory, 0) AS rate FROM inventory_metrics

anti_patterns.pattern: Counting units sold from partial shipments as full sell-through
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If a 100-unit order ships in 3 batches (40, 30, 30), and each shipment triggers a sell-through count, units_sold is double-counted — inflating rate and distorting inventory forecasting.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT shipment_id) AS shipments, SUM(oi.quantity) AS units_sold FROM shipments s JOIN order_items oi ON s.shipment_id = oi.shipment_id
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS orders, SUM(oi.quantity) AS units_sold FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'completed'

sql_assets.basic_sql: WITH inventory_start AS (SELECT sku_id, SUM(quantity) AS beginning_inventory FROM inventory_snapshots WHERE snapshot_date = '2024-01-01' GROUP BY sku_id), units_sold AS (SELECT oi.product_id AS sku_id, SUM(oi.quantity) AS units_sold FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' AND o.order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31' GROUP BY oi.product_id) SELECT s.sku_id, COALESCE(s.beginning_inventory, 0) AS beginning, COALESCE(u.units_sold, 0) AS sold, COALESCE(u.units_sold, 0) * 100.0 / NULLIF(s.beginning_inventory, 0) AS sell_through_rate FROM inventory_start s LEFT JOIN units_sold u ON s.sku_id = u.sku_id
sql_assets.intermediate_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', i.snapshot_date) AS month, p.category, SUM(i.quantity) FILTER (WHERE i.snapshot_date = '2024-01-01') AS beginning, SUM(oi.quantity) FILTER (WHERE o.status = 'completed' AND o.order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31') AS sold, SUM(oi.quantity) FILTER (WHERE o.status = 'completed' AND o.order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31') * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(i.quantity) FILTER (WHERE i.snapshot_date = '2024-01-01'), 0) AS sell_through_rate FROM inventory_snapshots i LEFT JOIN order_items oi ON i.sku_id = oi.product_id LEFT JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id LEFT JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id WHERE i.snapshot_date IN ('2024-01-01', '2024-01-31') GROUP BY 1, p.category ORDER BY 1, 5 DESC
sql_assets.advanced_sql:
WITH inventory_start AS (
  -- Beginning inventory: snapshot + incoming received before period end
  SELECT
    i.sku_id,
    COALESCE(SUM(i.quantity), 0) + COALESCE(SUM(CASE WHEN t.transaction_type = 'receipt' AND t.transaction_date <= '2024-01-31' THEN t.quantity END), 0) AS beginning_inventory
  FROM inventory_snapshots i
  LEFT JOIN inventory_transactions t ON i.sku_id = t.sku_id
  WHERE i.snapshot_date = '2024-01-01'
  GROUP BY i.sku_id
),
units_sold AS (
  -- Units sold: completed orders only
  SELECT
    oi.product_id AS sku_id,
    SUM(oi.quantity) AS units_sold
  FROM order_items oi
  JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
  WHERE o.status = 'completed' AND o.order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'
  GROUP BY oi.product_id
),
sell_through AS (
  SELECT
    s.sku_id,
    s.beginning_inventory,
    COALESCE(u.units_sold, 0) AS sold,
    ROUND(COALESCE(u.units_sold, 0) * 100.0 / NULLIF(s.beginning_inventory, 0), 2) AS sell_through_rate
  FROM inventory_start s
  LEFT JOIN units_sold u ON s.sku_id = u.sku_id
)
SELECT
  sku_id,
  beginning_inventory,
  sold,
  sell_through_rate,
  CASE WHEN sell_through_rate >= 80 THEN 'fast' WHEN sell_through_rate >= 40 THEN 'moderate' ELSE 'slow' END AS velocity_tier
FROM sell_through
ORDER BY sell_through_rate DESC

title: Dead Stock Rate
when_to_use: monitor inventory obsolescence and risk
identify candidates for markdowns, donations, or disposal
benchmark SKU or category inventory health over time
evaluate purchase planning accuracy
trigger auto-markdown or liquidation workflows
business_definition: Percentage of active SKUs (SKUs with inventory on hand) that have sold zero units in the last 90 days. Active SKUs are defined as having positive inventory on hand at period end.

metric_nature.what_it_measures: The proportion of inventory that is not moving — a direct indicator of forecast error, demand shifts, or merchandising issues.
metric_nature.what_it_does_not_measure: Value or age of dead stock. A SKU with $100 inventory is treated equally as one with $10,000 — this is a count metric, not a value metric.
metric_nature.common_misconception: Dead stock rate is not the same as obsolete inventory. Obsolescence is a value-based classification; dead stock is activity-based — some dead stock may be future classics or seasonal items held for reuse.

inflation_causes.cause: Using SKU count instead of inventory quantity count
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If the numerator counts SKUs with zero sales but denominator is *total* SKUs (including those with no inventory), the denominator inflates disproportionately — deflating rate. However, if zero-inventory SKUs are included in both, the *relative* effect may inflate rate. Net effect: inconsistent volatility.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT sku_id) AS total_skus, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN on_hand > 0 THEN sku_id END) AS active_skus, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN last_sale_date < '2024-01-01' THEN sku_id END) AS dead_skus FROM (SELECT sku_id, SUM(quantity) AS on_hand, MAX(o.order_date) AS last_sale_date FROM inventory_snapshots i LEFT JOIN order_items oi ON i.sku_id = oi.product_id LEFT JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE i.snapshot_date = '2024-01-31' GROUP BY sku_id) sub
inflation_causes.fix: Define active_skus strictly as SKUs with *positive inventory on hand* at period end, not just SKUs in system.

inflation_causes.cause: Including test or fraud orders in recent sales
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If a test order with many items is included in recent_sales, those SKUs are incorrectly marked as 'sold' — reducing the dead stock numerator and deflating rate.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT o.order_id, o.status, COUNT(DISTINCT oi.product_id) AS items FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id WHERE o.status = 'test' GROUP BY 1, 2 HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT oi.product_id) > 1 ORDER BY items DESC LIMIT 10
inflation_causes.fix: Filter to completed orders only and exclude test, fraud, and cancelled statuses.

inflation_causes.cause: Not filtering to current inventory on hand
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If inventory_snapshot uses historical snapshots (e.g., beginning of month), and SKUs have since sold out, they appear as active but are actually depleted — inflating dead_stock_rate artificially.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT sku_id, MAX(snapshot_date) AS last_snapshot, MAX(quantity) AS max_on_hand FROM inventory_snapshots WHERE snapshot_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY sku_id HAVING MAX(quantity) = 0 ORDER BY last_snapshot DESC LIMIT 10
inflation_causes.fix: Always use the *most recent* snapshot or real-time on-hand quantity (e.g., inventory_snapshots WHERE snapshot_date = {end_date}).

deflation_causes.cause: Excluding SKUs with negative inventory corrections
deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If negative inventory adjustments (e.g., shrinkage write-offs) result in zero or negative on-hand, and such SKUs are excluded from active_skus, their dead stock is missed — deflating rate.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT sku_id, SUM(quantity) AS net_on_hand FROM inventory_snapshots WHERE snapshot_date = '2024-01-31' GROUP BY sku_id HAVING SUM(quantity) <= 0 ORDER BY net_on_hand ASC LIMIT 10
deflation_causes.fix: Include SKUs with net_on_hand ≤ 0 as dead stock only if they have zero sales in 90 days; otherwise, treat negative as zero for calculation.

diagnostic_questions: Is the dead stock rate anomaly concentrated in specific categories, vendors, or SKUs?
Has a new product launch or category expansion increased SKU count without demand validation?
Are dead SKUs concentrated in older seasons or outdated styles?
Did inventory accuracy issues (e.g., cycle count discrepancies) affect on-hand counts?
Are dead stock rates higher in high-purchase categories or low-margin items?

causal_relationships.if: Dead stock rate up, sell_through_rate down
causal_relationships.then: inventory is sitting unsold — likely over-ordering, demand miscalculation, or merchandising misalignment

causal_relationships.if: Dead stock rate up, return_rate up
causal_relationships.then: inventory mismatches — likely sizing, quality, or description issues causing returns and stalled velocity

causal_relationships.if: Dead stock rate down, markdown_spend up
causal_relationships.then: markdown campaigns are clearing dead stock — likely effective promotion but margin-eroding

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Dead stock rate increases >8% MoM with stable purchase orders
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: A demand shift or competitive threat is reducing sell-through across categories — inventory is not moving, dead stock accumulates, and sell-through drops simultaneously.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', i.snapshot_date) AS month, COUNT(DISTINCT i.sku_id) AS active_skus, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_date >= DATEADD(day, -90, i.snapshot_date) THEN o.order_id END) AS recent_orders, COUNT(DISTINCT i.sku_id) FILTER (WHERE o.order_id IS NULL) AS dead_skus FROM inventory_snapshots i LEFT JOIN order_items oi ON i.sku_id = oi.product_id LEFT JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE i.snapshot_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY month

cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: Markdown promotions or clearance events are clearing older dead stock, reducing dead stock count, but new overstock is accumulating — likely short-term inventory optimization at the cost of long-term balance.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month, COUNT(DISTINCT oi.product_id) AS skus_sold, SUM(oi.quantity) AS units, SUM(CASE WHEN oi.order_date >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31') THEN oi.quantity END) AS units_90d, SUM(CASE WHEN oi.order_date < DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31') THEN oi.quantity END) AS units_old FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' AND o.order_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY month

anti_patterns.pattern: Defining dead stock as 'no sales ever' instead of 'no sales in 90 days'
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If dead stock excludes all SKUs with zero lifetime sales, healthy seasonal or infrequent items (e.g., holiday decor) are misclassified — inflating rate and masking true obsolescence.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN last_sale_date IS NULL THEN sku_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT sku_id), 0) AS rate FROM (SELECT sku_id, MAX(order_date) AS last_sale_date FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id GROUP BY sku_id) sub
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN last_sale_date < DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31') THEN sku_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN quantity > 0 THEN sku_id END), 0) AS rate FROM inventory_snapshots i LEFT JOIN (SELECT product_id AS sku_id, MAX(order_date) AS last_sale_date FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id GROUP BY product_id) r ON i.sku_id = r.sku_id WHERE i.snapshot_date = '2024-01-31'

anti_patterns.pattern: Counting dead stock as a percentage of *total* SKUs
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If denominator includes SKUs with zero inventory, dead stock appears higher — because non-active SKUs (e.g., discontinued) are incorrectly counted as dead stock.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN last_sale_date < DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31') THEN sku_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT sku_id), 0) AS rate FROM inventory_snapshots i LEFT JOIN (SELECT product_id AS sku_id, MAX(order_date) AS last_sale_date FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id GROUP BY product_id) r ON i.sku_id = r.sku_id GROUP BY 1
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN last_sale_date < DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31') THEN sku_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN quantity > 0 THEN sku_id END), 0) AS rate FROM inventory_snapshots i LEFT JOIN (SELECT product_id AS sku_id, MAX(order_date) AS last_sale_date FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id GROUP BY product_id) r ON i.sku_id = r.sku_id WHERE i.snapshot_date = '2024-01-31'

sql_assets.basic_sql: WITH active_skus AS (SELECT DISTINCT sku_id FROM inventory_snapshots WHERE snapshot_date = '2024-01-31' AND quantity > 0), recent_sales AS (SELECT DISTINCT oi.product_id AS sku_id FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' AND o.order_date >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31')), inventory_counts AS (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT a.sku_id) AS active_count FROM active_skus a), dead_counts AS (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT a.sku_id) AS dead_count FROM active_skus a LEFT JOIN recent_sales r ON a.sku_id = r.sku_id WHERE r.sku_id IS NULL) SELECT dc.dead_count, ic.active_count, dc.dead_count * 100.0 / NULLIF(ic.active_count, 0) AS dead_stock_rate FROM dead_counts dc, inventory_counts ic
sql_assets.intermediate_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', i.snapshot_date) AS month, p.category, COUNT(DISTINCT i.sku_id) FILTER (WHERE i.quantity > 0) AS active_skus, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN r.last_sale_date < DATEADD(day, -90, i.snapshot_date) THEN i.sku_id END) AS dead_skus, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN r.last_sale_date < DATEADD(day, -90, i.snapshot_date) THEN i.sku_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN i.quantity > 0 THEN i.sku_id END), 0) AS dead_stock_rate FROM inventory_snapshots i LEFT JOIN (SELECT product_id AS sku_id, MAX(order_date) AS last_sale_date FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id GROUP BY product_id) r ON i.sku_id = r.sku_id JOIN products p ON i.sku_id = p.sku_id WHERE i.snapshot_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1, p.category ORDER BY 1, 5 DESC
sql_assets.advanced_sql:
WITH active_skus AS (
  -- Active SKUs: on-hand > 0 at period end
  SELECT sku_id
  FROM inventory_snapshots
  WHERE snapshot_date = '2024-01-31' AND quantity > 0
),
recent_sales AS (
  -- Recent sales: completed orders in last 90 days
  SELECT DISTINCT oi.product_id AS sku_id
  FROM order_items oi
  JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
  WHERE o.status = 'completed' AND o.order_date >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31')
),
dead_stock AS (
  -- Dead stock: active SKUs with no recent sales
  SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT a.sku_id) AS dead_count
  FROM active_skus a
  LEFT JOIN recent_sales r ON a.sku_id = r.sku_id
  WHERE r.sku_id IS NULL
),
total_active AS (
  SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT sku_id) AS active_count FROM active_skus
),
category_breakdown AS (
  SELECT p.category, COUNT(DISTINCT a.sku_id) AS active_skus, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN r.sku_id IS NULL THEN a.sku_id END) AS dead_skus
  FROM active_skus a
  LEFT JOIN products p ON a.sku_id = p.sku_id
  LEFT JOIN recent_sales r ON a.sku_id = r.sku_id
  GROUP BY p.category
)
SELECT
  ds.dead_count,
  ta.active_count,
  ROUND(ds.dead_count * 100.0 / NULLIF(ta.active_count, 0), 2) AS dead_stock_rate,
  cb.category,
  cb.active_skus,
  cb.dead_skus,
  ROUND(cb.dead_skus * 100.0 / NULLIF(cb.active_skus, 0), 2) AS category_dead_stock_rate
FROM dead_stock ds
CROSS JOIN total_active ta
CROSS JOIN category_breakdown cb
ORDER BY cb.dead_skus DESC

title: Overstock Index
when_to_use: assess inventory excess and planning accuracy
trigger markdown or liquidation workflows
benchmark inventory balance across categories or vendors
optimize reorder points and purchase planning
evaluate impact of demand forecast changes
business_definition: Ratio of current inventory on hand to forward demand forecast for the next 90 days, expressed as a multiplier. A value >1 indicates overstock; <1 indicates understock. Forward demand is forecast using 30-day trailing sell-through * 3 (assuming linear demand).

metric_nature.what_it_measures: The balance between current stock and projected demand — a forward-looking indicator of inventory surplus or shortage.
metric_nature.what_it_does_not_measure: Revenue or profit impact of overstock. High overstock may be strategic (e.g., seasonal build) and profitable.
metric_nature.common_misconception: Overstock index is not the same as inventory turnover. Turnover measures sales velocity; overstock index compares stock to *future* demand — it’s a stock-to-demand ratio, not a flow ratio.

inflation_causes.cause: Using trailing demand instead of forward demand forecast
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If forward demand is defined as *past* 90-day sales (not forecast), and recent demand is declining, the denominator remains high while stock is reduced — artificially inflating overstock index.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month, SUM(oi.quantity) AS units_sold FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY month DESC LIMIT 3
inflation_causes.fix: Use a *forecast* for forward demand (e.g., trailing 30 days * 3) instead of actual trailing 90 days — or adjust forecast for known trends.

inflation_causes.cause: Not excluding reserved or allocated inventory from on-hand
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If reserved inventory (e.g., pending orders, QC holds) is included in on-hand, but not subtracted, the numerator is inflated — artificially increasing overstock index.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT sku_id, SUM(quantity) AS total, SUM(CASE WHEN allocation_type = 'reserved' THEN quantity END) AS reserved FROM inventory_snapshots WHERE snapshot_date = '2024-01-31' GROUP BY sku_id HAVING SUM(CASE WHEN allocation_type = 'reserved' THEN quantity END) > 0 ORDER BY reserved DESC LIMIT 10
inflation_causes.fix: Subtract reserved and allocated quantities before computing on_hand: `on_hand = SUM(quantity) - SUM(CASE WHEN allocation_type IN ('reserved','allocated') THEN quantity END)`.

inflation_causes.cause: Including SKUs with zero demand in forward_demand
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If SKUs with zero sales in the trailing period are included in forward_demand with demand_90d = 0, and on_hand > 0, the overstock_index = INFINITY — distorting averages and inflating overall index.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT sku_id, SUM(oi.quantity) AS sales_30d FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' AND o.order_date >= DATEADD(day, -30, '2024-01-31') GROUP BY sku_id HAVING SUM(oi.quantity) = 0 ORDER BY sku_id LIMIT 10
inflation_causes.fix: Exclude SKUs with zero demand from overstock calculation or replace zero demand with a minimum forecast (e.g., demand_90d = 1).

deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If negative inventory (e.g., adjustments, errors) is excluded from on_hand, but those SKUs have demand, their overstock is understated — deflating index.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT sku_id, SUM(quantity) AS net_on_hand FROM inventory_snapshots WHERE snapshot_date = '2024-01-31' GROUP BY sku_id HAVING SUM(quantity) < 0 ORDER BY net_on_hand ASC LIMIT 10
deflation_causes.fix: Treat negative inventory as zero for on_hand calculation, but include in overstock logic only if demand > 0 (to flag stockout risk).

diagnostic_questions: Is the overstock anomaly concentrated in specific categories, vendors, or SKUs?
Has a recent demand forecast revision changed forward demand estimates?
Are overstock items concentrated in seasonal or promotional SKUs?
Did inventory accuracy issues (e.g., cycle count discrepancies) affect on-hand counts?
Are overstock levels higher in low-turnover or slow-selling categories?

causal_relationships.then: inventory accumulation without demand — likely over-ordering or demand miscalculation
inventory not only overstocked but also unsold — likely poor demand forecasting or product-market fit
inventory reduction matched by demand — likely successful clearance or improved planning

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Overstock index increases >20% WoW with stable sell-through rate
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: A planned inventory build (e.g., for holiday season, bulk purchase) is increasing on-hand without immediate demand change — likely proactive supply chain optimization.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', i.snapshot_date) AS week, SUM(i.quantity) AS on_hand, COUNT(DISTINCT oi.order_id) AS orders_30d, COUNT(DISTINCT po.order_id) AS purchase_orders_next_90d FROM inventory_snapshots i LEFT JOIN order_items oi ON i.sku_id = oi.product_id LEFT JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id LEFT JOIN purchase_orders po ON i.sku_id = po.sku_id WHERE i.snapshot_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY week

cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: Markdown campaigns are clearing overstock faster than new dead stock accumulates — likely short-term optimization at the cost of long-term balance.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month, COUNT(DISTINCT oi.product_id) AS skus_sold, SUM(oi.quantity) AS units, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_date >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31') THEN oi.product_id END) AS units_90d, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_date < DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31') THEN oi.product_id END) AS units_old FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' AND o.order_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY month

anti_patterns.pattern: Defining overstock as current inventory / past 90-day sales
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If forward demand is not *forecast*, but past 90-day sales, and demand is declining, overstock index appears inflated — because denominator shrinks faster than stock.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT on_hand / NULLIF((SELECT SUM(oi.quantity) FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' AND o.order_date >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31')), 0) AS index FROM inventory_snapshots WHERE snapshot_date = '2024-01-31'
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT on_hand / NULLIF((SELECT SUM(oi.quantity) * 3 FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' AND o.order_date >= DATEADD(day, -30, '2024-01-31')), 0) AS index FROM inventory_snapshots WHERE snapshot_date = '2024-01-31'

anti_patterns.pattern: Using average inventory instead of period-end on-hand
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If overstock uses average inventory (beginning + ending)/2, and stock fluctuates, the index averages out volatility — masking short-term overstock spikes.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT (SUM(oi.quantity) FILTER (WHERE i.snapshot_date = '2024-01-01') + SUM(oi.quantity) FILTER (WHERE i.snapshot_date = '2024-01-31')) / 2 / NULLIF(d.demand_90d, 0) AS index FROM inventory_snapshots i CROSS JOIN forward_demand d
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT SUM(oi.quantity) FILTER (WHERE i.snapshot_date = '2024-01-31') / NULLIF(d.demand_90d, 0) AS index FROM inventory_snapshots i CROSS JOIN forward_demand d

sql_assets.basic_sql: WITH inventory_on_hand AS (SELECT sku_id, SUM(quantity) AS on_hand FROM inventory_snapshots WHERE snapshot_date = '2024-01-31' GROUP BY sku_id), forward_demand AS (SELECT oi.product_id AS sku_id, SUM(oi.quantity) * 3 AS demand_90d FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' AND o.order_date >= DATEADD(day, -30, '2024-01-31') GROUP BY oi.product_id), overstock AS (SELECT i.sku_id, COALESCE(i.on_hand, 0) AS on_hand, COALESCE(d.demand_90d, 1) AS demand, COALESCE(i.on_hand, 0) / NULLIF(d.demand_90d, 1) AS overstock_index FROM inventory_on_hand i LEFT JOIN forward_demand d ON i.sku_id = d.sku_id) SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN overstock_index > 1 THEN sku_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT sku_id), 0) AS overstock_index_pct, AVG(overstock_index) AS avg_overstock_index FROM overstock
sql_assets.intermediate_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', i.snapshot_date) AS month, p.category, SUM(i.quantity) AS on_hand, COUNT(DISTINCT oi.product_id) FILTER (WHERE o.order_date >= DATEADD(day, -30, i.snapshot_date)) * 3 AS demand_90d, SUM(i.quantity) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT oi.product_id) FILTER (WHERE o.order_date >= DATEADD(day, -30, i.snapshot_date)) * 3, 1) AS overstock_index FROM inventory_snapshots i LEFT JOIN order_items oi ON i.sku_id = oi.product_id LEFT JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id JOIN products p ON i.sku_id = p.product_id WHERE i.snapshot_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1, p.category ORDER BY 1, 5 DESC
sql_assets.advanced_sql:
WITH inventory_on_hand AS (
  -- Current inventory: on-hand at period end, excluding reserved
  SELECT
    sku_id,
    SUM(quantity) - COALESCE(SUM(CASE WHEN allocation_type IN ('reserved','allocated') THEN quantity END), 0) AS on_hand
  FROM inventory_snapshots
  WHERE snapshot_date = '2024-01-31'
  GROUP BY sku_id
),
forward_demand AS (
  -- Forward demand: trailing 30 days * 3 (linear forecast)
  SELECT
    oi.product_id AS sku_id,
    SUM(oi.quantity) * 3 AS demand_90d
  FROM order_items oi
  JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
  WHERE o.status = 'completed' AND o.order_date >= DATEADD(day, -30, '2024-01-31')
  GROUP BY oi.product_id
),
overstock AS (
  SELECT
    i.sku_id,
    COALESCE(i.on_hand, 0) AS on_hand,
    COALESCE(d.demand_90d, 1) AS demand_90d,
    ROUND(COALESCE(i.on_hand, 0) / NULLIF(d.demand_90d, 1), 2) AS overstock_index
  FROM inventory_on_hand i
  LEFT JOIN forward_demand d ON i.sku_id = d.sku_id
)
SELECT
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN overstock_index > 1 THEN sku_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT sku_id), 0) AS overstock_index_pct,
  ROUND(AVG(overstock_index), 2) AS avg_overstock_index,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN overstock_index > 1 THEN sku_id END) AS overstock_skus,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN overstock_index < 0.5 THEN sku_id END) AS understock_skus
FROM overstock

title: Markdown Depth
when_to_use: assess promotional intensity and margin impact
benchmark markdown depth across categories or vendors
optimize markdown timing and depth for inventory clearance
evaluate impact of markdowns on revenue and margin
trigger automated markdown workflows based on inventory age
business_definition: Average percentage reduction from original listed price applied to marked-down items, calculated as (original_price - sale_price) / original_price * 100. Only includes items with a markdown applied (sale_price < original_price).

metric_nature.what_it_measures: How aggressively prices are reduced — a proxy for promotional intensity and inventory clearance urgency.
metric_nature.what_it_does_not_measure: Revenue or margin impact of markdowns. A deep markdown may clear inventory quickly but erode margins significantly.
metric_nature.common_misconception: Markdown depth is not the same as discount rate. Discount rate includes all discounts (including full-price promotions); markdown depth focuses on *reduced-price items* relative to original MSRP.

template: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN m.markdown_price < m.original_price THEN oi.order_id END) AS markdown_orders, COUNT(DISTINCT oi.order_id) AS total_orders, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN m.markdown_price < m.original_price THEN oi.order_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT oi.order_id), 0) AS markdown_rate, AVG(CASE WHEN m.markdown_price < m.original_price THEN (m.original_price - m.markdown_price) * 100.0 / NULLIF(m.original_price, 0) END) AS avg_markdown_depth FROM order_items oi JOIN markdown_events m ON oi.order_item_id = m.order_item_id JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' AND o.order_date BETWEEN {start_date} AND {end_date}

inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If listed_price is already discounted (e.g., net price after promo), and original_price is not tracked separately, markdown depth is calculated against a lower baseline — inflating depth.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT AVG(listed_price) AS avg_listed, AVG(original_price) AS avg_original FROM markdown_events WHERE markdown_date >= '2024-01-01'
inflation_causes.fix: Ensure original_price reflects MSRP or *true* pre-markdown price before calculating depth.

inflation_causes.cause: Including non-markdown items (e.g., clearance with no original price)
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If original_price = 0 or NULL for clearance items, and depth is calculated as (original_price - sale_price) / original_price, those items are excluded. But if they’re counted as full markdowns, average depth inflates.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN original_price <= 0 THEN order_item_id END) AS zero_price_markdowns, COUNT(DISTINCT order_item_id) AS total_markdowns FROM markdown_events WHERE markdown_date >= '2024-01-01'
inflation_causes.fix: Filter to items with original_price > 0 and sale_price < original_price before calculating depth.

inflation_causes.cause: Counting markdowns per item instead of per order
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If multiple items in one order are markdowns, and markdown_rate is calculated as `COUNT(markdown_items) / COUNT(items)`, the rate exceeds 100% — but if average depth is calculated per item, it remains valid. Net effect: rate inflation only if rate uses *items*, not *orders*.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT order_id, COUNT(DISTINCT order_item_id) AS items, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN m.markdown_price < m.original_price THEN order_item_id END) AS markdown_items FROM order_items oi LEFT JOIN markdown_events m ON oi.order_item_id = m.order_item_id JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' GROUP BY 1 HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN m.markdown_price < m.original_price THEN order_item_id END) > 0 ORDER BY markdown_items DESC LIMIT 10
inflation_causes.fix: Calculate markdown_rate at order level: `COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN markdown_applied = true THEN order_id END) / COUNT(DISTINCT order_id)`.

deflation_causes.cause: Excluding items with original_price > sale_price but sale_price = original_price
deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If markdown_depth only includes items where sale_price < original_price, but original_price = sale_price due to a *negative* markdown (e.g., price increase before markdown), depth is deflated.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN original_price > sale_price AND sale_price >= original_price THEN order_item_id END) AS zero_net_markdowns FROM markdown_events WHERE markdown_date >= '2024-01-01'
deflation_causes.fix: Include only items where a markdown *event* occurred (e.g., m.markdown_type IS NOT NULL), not just price differential.

diagnostic_questions: Is the markdown depth anomaly concentrated in specific categories, vendors, or SKUs?
Has a new markdown automation rule (e.g., X days since last markdown) been introduced?
Are deeper markdowns correlated with slower sell-through or higher dead stock?
Did markdown timing or depth change seasonally or after inventory events?
Are markdowns driving additional volume or just margin erosion?

causal_relationships.then: deeper discounts driving faster sell-through — likely effective clearance or inventory optimization
deeper discounts eroding margins — likely trade-off between velocity and profitability
shallower discounts failing to clear inventory — likely markdown timing or pricing misalignment

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Markdown depth increases >10pp MoM with stable sell-through rate
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: A shift toward deeper, less frequent markdowns (e.g., later-stage clearance vs early promotional discounts) is increasing average depth but reducing overall discount rate.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', m.markdown_date) AS month, COUNT(DISTINCT m.order_item_id) AS markdown_items, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN m.markdown_price < m.original_price THEN m.order_item_id END) AS active_markdowns, AVG(CASE WHEN m.markdown_price < m.original_price THEN (m.original_price - m.markdown_price) * 100.0 / NULLIF(m.original_price, 0) END) AS avg_depth, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN m.markdown_price < m.original_price THEN m.order_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT m.order_id), 0) AS discount_rate FROM markdown_events m JOIN orders o ON m.order_id = o.order_id WHERE m.markdown_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY month

cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: Shallower discounts are failing to clear slow-moving inventory — likely markdown timing, depth thresholds, or promotion strategy issues.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', m.markdown_date) AS month, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN m.markdown_price < m.original_price THEN m.order_item_id END) AS active_markdowns, AVG(CASE WHEN m.markdown_price < m.original_price THEN (m.original_price - m.markdown_price) * 100.0 / NULLIF(m.original_price, 0) END) AS avg_depth FROM markdown_events m JOIN orders o ON m.order_id = o.order_id WHERE m.markdown_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY month

anti_patterns.pattern: Using listed_price instead of original_price in denominator
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If listed_price already reflects a prior discount, markdown depth is inflated — because the baseline is artificially low.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT AVG((listed_price - net_price) * 100.0 / NULLIF(listed_price, 0)) AS depth FROM order_items WHERE net_price < listed_price
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT AVG((original_price - markdown_price) * 100.0 / NULLIF(original_price, 0)) AS depth FROM markdown_events WHERE markdown_price < original_price

anti_patterns.pattern: Calculating average depth per *order* instead of per *markdown event*
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If one order has one deep discount and nine full-price items, averaging by order deflates depth — because the denominator includes non-markdown items.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT AVG((original_price - markdown_price) * 100.0 / NULLIF(original_price, 0)) AS depth FROM (SELECT order_id, MAX(original_price) AS original, MAX(markdown_price) AS markdown FROM markdown_events GROUP BY order_id) sub
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT AVG((original_price - markdown_price) * 100.0 / NULLIF(original_price, 0)) AS depth FROM markdown_events WHERE markdown_price < original_price

sql_assets.basic_sql: SELECT AVG(CASE WHEN m.markdown_price < m.original_price THEN (m.original_price - m.markdown_price) * 100.0 / NULLIF(m.original_price, 0) END) AS avg_markdown_depth FROM markdown_events m JOIN orders o ON m.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' AND o.order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'
sql_assets.intermediate_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', m.markdown_date) AS month, p.category, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN m.markdown_price < m.original_price THEN m.order_item_id END) AS markdown_items, AVG(CASE WHEN m.markdown_price < m.original_price THEN (m.original_price - m.markdown_price) * 100.0 / NULLIF(m.original_price, 0) END) AS avg_depth, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN m.markdown_price < m.original_price THEN m.order_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT m.order_id), 0) AS markdown_rate FROM markdown_events m JOIN order_items oi ON m.order_item_id = oi.order_item_id JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id JOIN orders o ON m.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' AND m.markdown_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1, p.category ORDER BY 1, 4 DESC
sql_assets.advanced_sql:
WITH markdown_events_filtered AS (
  -- Grain: markdown events with valid original_price
  SELECT
    m.order_id,
    m.order_item_id,
    m.original_price,
    m.markdown_price,
    m.markdown_date,
    p.category
  FROM markdown_events m
  JOIN order_items oi ON m.order_item_id = oi.order_item_id
  JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
  JOIN orders o ON m.order_id = o.order_id
  WHERE o.status = 'completed'
    AND m.markdown_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'
    AND m.original_price > 0
    AND m.markdown_price < m.original_price
),
markdown_metrics AS (
  SELECT
    COUNT(DISTINCT order_item_id) AS markdown_items,
    COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS markdown_orders,
    ROUND(AVG((original_price - markdown_price) * 100.0 / NULLIF(original_price, 0)), 2) AS avg_markdown_depth_pct,
    ROUND(AVG(original_price - markdown_price), 2) AS avg_markdown_dollar
  FROM markdown_events_filtered
)
SELECT
  markdown_items,
  markdown_orders,
  avg_markdown_depth_pct,
  avg_markdown_dollar,
  CASE
    WHEN avg_markdown_depth_pct >= 40 THEN 'deep'
    WHEN avg_markdown_depth_pct >= 20 THEN 'moderate'
    ELSE 'light'
  END AS depth_tier
FROM markdown_metrics

title: New Product Revenue Share
when_to_use: assess innovation pipeline impact on revenue
benchmark new product performance against historical launches
evaluate new product ROI and launch effectiveness
optimize new product investment and marketing spend
identify high-performing or underperforming new SKUs
business_definition: Percentage of net revenue in the period derived from products launched in the last 90 days. A new product is defined as one with first order_date >= {start_date} - 90 days. Net revenue is GMV minus refunds and discounts.

metric_nature.what_it_measures: The contribution of new products to overall revenue — a measure of innovation-driven growth and launch effectiveness.
metric_nature.what_it_does_not_measure: Profitability or customer acquisition from new products. New products may drive revenue but erode margins or fail to acquire customers.
metric_nature.common_misconception: New product revenue share is not the same as new customer revenue. It tracks *product novelty*, not *customer novelty* — repeat customers may purchase new products.

inflation_causes.cause: Using first order_date from *all* orders instead of first *completed* order
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If cancelled or test orders are included in first order_date, a product’s launch date appears earlier — but if those orders are excluded from revenue, new product revenue is understated — net effect: inconsistent volatility depending on timing.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT product_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN status = 'cancelled' THEN order_id END) AS cancelled_orders, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN status = 'completed' THEN order_id END) AS completed_orders FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id GROUP BY 1 HAVING MIN(order_date) >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31') ORDER BY first_order DESC LIMIT 10
inflation_causes.fix: Filter to completed orders only when defining new products: `MIN(order_date) WHERE status = 'completed'`.

inflation_causes.cause: Counting revenue from *all* time for new products instead of period-only
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If new_product_revenue aggregates lifetime sales, and period revenue is only this quarter, new product share appears inflated — because new products contribute sales from prior periods too.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT product_id, SUM(CASE WHEN order_date >= '2024-01-01' THEN nr.net_revenue END) AS period_revenue, SUM(nr.net_revenue) AS lifetime_revenue FROM order_items oi JOIN net_revenue_daily nr ON oi.order_id = nr.order_id JOIN new_products np ON oi.product_id = np.product_id WHERE np.launch_date >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31') GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY lifetime_revenue DESC LIMIT 10
inflation_causes.fix: Restrict new_product_revenue to period only: `SUM(nr.net_revenue) WHERE nr.order_date BETWEEN {start_date} AND {end_date}`.

inflation_causes.cause: Including pre-orders or预售 as new products
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If pre-order revenue is attributed to launch_date = first order_date (not first shipment), new product revenue includes non-fulfilled revenue — inflating share if pre-orders are not recognized.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.status = 'pre_order' THEN oi.order_id END) AS pre_orders, SUM(nr.net_revenue) FILTER (WHERE o.status = 'pre_order') AS pre_order_revenue FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id JOIN net_revenue_daily nr ON oi.order_id = nr.order_id JOIN new_products np ON oi.product_id = np.product_id
inflation_causes.fix: Attribute new product revenue to shipment_date or fulfillment_date, not order_date — only count completed or shipped revenue.

deflation_causes.cause: Excluding new products with zero sales in period
deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If new_product_revenue only includes products with sales in period, but launch_date is set at period start, products launched at period end are excluded — deflating share.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT product_id, launch_date, COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) FILTER (WHERE order_date >= launch_date) AS orders, SUM(net_revenue) FILTER (WHERE order_date >= launch_date) AS revenue FROM new_products np LEFT JOIN order_items oi ON np.product_id = oi.product_id LEFT JOIN net_revenue_daily nr ON oi.order_id = nr.order_id GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY launch_date DESC LIMIT 10
deflation_causes.fix: Include all new products defined by launch_date (e.g., within 90 days of period end) regardless of period sales — use period revenue only.

diagnostic_questions: Is the new product revenue share anomaly concentrated in specific categories, vendors, or SKUs?
Has a new product launch or cohort gone live recently?
Are new products performing better or worse than historical launches?
Did product development or marketing spend change affecting new product success?
Are new product margins higher or lower than legacy products?

causal_relationships.if: New product revenue share up, net_revenue up
causal_relationships.then: successful new product launches driving top-line growth — likely innovative or well-marketed items

causal_relationships.if: New product revenue share up, gross_profit_per_order down
causal_relationships.then: new products have lower margins — likely competitive pricing or higher acquisition costs

causal_relationships.if: New product revenue share down, new_product_count up
causal_relationships.then: more new products but lower performance — likely launch quality issues or marketing misalignment

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: New product revenue share increases >15pp WoW with flat total revenue
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: High-margin new products (e.g., premium SKUs, bundled items) are driving revenue and margin — likely successful innovation or strategic product mix shift.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN np.launch_date >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31') THEN oi.product_id END) AS new_products, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN np.launch_date >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31') THEN oi.order_id END) AS new_orders, SUM(nr.net_revenue) FILTER (WHERE np.launch_date >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31')) AS new_product_revenue, SUM(nr.net_revenue) AS total_revenue FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id JOIN net_revenue_daily nr ON oi.order_id = nr.order_id LEFT JOIN (SELECT product_id, MIN(order_date) AS launch_date FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' GROUP BY product_id HAVING MIN(order_date) >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31')) np ON oi.product_id = np.product_id WHERE o.order_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY month

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: New product revenue share drops MoM with stable new_product_count
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: Lower-quality new products are returning at higher rates — likely sizing, quality, or description issues undermining new product success despite volume.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN np.launch_date >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31') THEN oi.product_id END) AS new_products, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN r.order_id IS NOT NULL AND np.launch_date >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31') THEN r.order_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN np.launch_date >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31') THEN oi.order_id END), 0) AS new_product_return_rate FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id LEFT JOIN returns r ON oi.order_id = r.order_id LEFT JOIN (SELECT product_id, MIN(order_date) AS launch_date FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' GROUP BY product_id HAVING MIN(order_date) >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31')) np ON oi.product_id = np.product_id WHERE o.order_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY month

anti_patterns.pattern: Using first *order_item* creation date instead of first *order_completion* date
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If new product is defined by order_item creation but launch_date uses first order_date *before* completion, new products are misidentified — including cancelled or pre-order items.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT product_id, MIN(order_date) AS launch_date FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id GROUP BY 1 HAVING MIN(order_date) >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31')
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT product_id, MIN(order_date) AS launch_date FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' GROUP BY 1 HAVING MIN(order_date) >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31')

anti_patterns.pattern: Counting revenue from *all* SKUs launched in last 90 days, regardless of period sales
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If new_product_revenue uses *total* lifetime revenue, and period revenue is only this quarter, new product share appears inflated — because legacy products’ lifetime sales are included.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT SUM(nr.net_revenue) AS new_product_revenue FROM order_items oi JOIN new_products np ON oi.product_id = np.product_id JOIN net_revenue_daily nr ON oi.order_id = nr.order_id
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT SUM(nr.net_revenue) AS new_product_revenue FROM order_items oi JOIN new_products np ON oi.product_id = np.product_id JOIN net_revenue_daily nr ON oi.order_id = nr.order_id WHERE nr.order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'

sql_assets.basic_sql: WITH new_products AS (SELECT product_id, MIN(order_date) AS launch_date FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' GROUP BY product_id HAVING MIN(order_date) >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31')), new_product_revenue AS (SELECT SUM(nr.net_revenue) AS new_product_revenue FROM order_items oi JOIN new_products np ON oi.product_id = np.product_id JOIN net_revenue_daily nr ON oi.order_id = nr.order_id WHERE nr.order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'), total_revenue AS (SELECT SUM(net_revenue) AS total_revenue FROM net_revenue_daily WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31') SELECT COALESCE(npr.new_product_revenue, 0) AS new_product_revenue, tr.total_revenue, COALESCE(npr.new_product_revenue, 0) * 100.0 / NULLIF(tr.total_revenue, 0) AS new_product_revenue_share FROM new_product_revenue npr, total_revenue tr
sql_assets.intermediate_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month, p.category, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN np.launch_date >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31') THEN oi.product_id END) AS new_products, SUM(nr.net_revenue) FILTER (WHERE np.launch_date >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31')) AS new_product_revenue, SUM(nr.net_revenue) AS total_revenue, SUM(nr.net_revenue) FILTER (WHERE np.launch_date >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31')) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(nr.net_revenue), 0) AS share FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id JOIN net_revenue_daily nr ON oi.order_id = nr.order_id JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id LEFT JOIN (SELECT product_id, MIN(order_date) AS launch_date FROM order_items oi JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id WHERE o.status = 'completed' GROUP BY product_id HAVING MIN(order_date) >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31')) np ON oi.product_id = np.product_id WHERE o.order_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY 1, p.category ORDER BY 1, 6 DESC
sql_assets.advanced_sql:
WITH new_products AS (
  -- Grain: products launched in last 90 days (based on first completed order)
  SELECT
    product_id,
    MIN(order_date) AS launch_date
  FROM order_items oi
  JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
  WHERE o.status = 'completed'
  GROUP BY product_id
  HAVING MIN(order_date) >= DATEADD(day, -90, '2024-01-31')
),
new_product_revenue AS (
  -- Period revenue only from new products
  SELECT
    SUM(nr.net_revenue) AS new_product_revenue
  FROM order_items oi
  JOIN new_products np ON oi.product_id = np.product_id
  JOIN net_revenue_daily nr ON oi.order_id = nr.order_id
  WHERE nr.order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'
),
total_revenue AS (
  SELECT SUM(net_revenue) AS total_revenue FROM net_revenue_daily WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'
),
product_category AS (
  SELECT
    p.category,
    COUNT(DISTINCT np.product_id) AS new_product_count,
    SUM(nr.net_revenue) AS new_product_revenue
  FROM new_products np
  JOIN order_items oi ON np.product_id = oi.product_id
  JOIN net_revenue_daily nr ON oi.order_id = nr.order_id
  JOIN products p ON np.product_id = p.product_id
  WHERE nr.order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'
  GROUP BY p.category
)
SELECT
  COALESCE(npr.new_product_revenue, 0) AS total_new_product_revenue,
  tr.total_revenue,
  ROUND(COALESCE(npr.new_product_revenue, 0) * 100.0 / NULLIF(tr.total_revenue, 0), 2) AS new_product_revenue_share,
  pc.category,
  pc.new_product_count,
  pc.new_product_revenue,
  ROUND(pc.new_product_revenue * 100.0 / NULLIF(tr.total_revenue, 0), 2) AS category_share
FROM new_product_revenue npr
CROSS JOIN total_revenue tr
CROSS JOIN product_category pc
ORDER BY pc.new_product_revenue DESC
